name: Periodic Table of Elements
description: Master the building blocks of the universe — from hydrogen to uranium. Learn what gives rubies their red glow, why gold never tarnishes, and which element was discovered by boiling urine. Every card unlocks a piece of nature's fundamental puzzle.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element makes up 75% of all normal matter in the universe?
facts.explanation: Hydrogen is the lightest and simplest element, with just one proton. It is so abundant that three-quarters of all normal matter in the universe is hydrogen — every star is mostly hydrogen. Source: Wikipedia/Hydrogen
facts.statement: Hydrogen makes up 75% of all normal matter in the universe.
facts.wowFactor: Three out of every four atoms of normal matter in the entire universe are hydrogen — every star, nebula, and gas cloud is mostly made of it.
facts.visualDescription: A single glowing hydrogen atom drifts through deep space, surrounded by countless stars and galaxies, each one a burning ball of the same element

variants.question: Hydrogen holds which distinction among all elements?
Which element is the lightest in the periodic table?
True or false: Hydrogen is the most abundant element in the universe.
With atomic number 1, ___ is the lightest element.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the atomic number of Hydrogen?
facts.explanation: Hydrogen has atomic number 1 — it has exactly one proton in its nucleus, making it the simplest atom possible. The most common isotope has no neutrons at all. Source: Wikipedia/Hydrogen
facts.statement: Hydrogen has atomic number 1.
facts.wowFactor: Hydrogen is the only element whose most common form has no neutrons at all — just one proton and one electron.
facts.visualDescription: A single proton glows gold in a void, with one electron orbiting it in a perfect ring — the simplest atom in existence

variants.question: Hydrogen is the only common element whose atom has no what?
True or false: The most common hydrogen atom has no neutrons.
Hydrogen has atomic number ___, meaning it has just one proton.
Which element has atomic number 1?

facts.quizQuestion: Which element forms both the hardest natural mineral and a soft lubricant?
facts.explanation: Carbon forms diamond (the hardest natural substance) and graphite (a soft lubricant used in pencils) — two entirely different materials made of exactly the same element, just arranged differently. Source: Wikipedia/Carbon
facts.statement: Carbon forms both diamond and graphite, depending on how its atoms are arranged.
facts.wowFactor: Diamond and pencil lead are literally the same element — just carbon atoms arranged in different patterns. One cuts glass; the other smears on paper.
facts.visualDescription: Side by side: a brilliant sparkling diamond and a stub of graphite pencil, both labeled 'Carbon' — identical atoms, completely different worlds

variants.question: Diamond and graphite are both pure forms of which element?
What are different structural forms of the same element called?
True or false: Diamond and graphite are both made entirely of carbon atoms.
Diamond and graphite are allotropes of ___, the basis of all organic chemistry.

facts.quizQuestion: Which gas makes up 78% of Earth's atmosphere?
facts.explanation: Nitrogen (N2) makes up about 78% of Earth's atmosphere. Despite being so common in the air, nitrogen gas is surprisingly unreactive — yet as compounds like TNT and dynamite, it is explosively energetic. Source: Wikipedia/Nitrogen
facts.statement: Nitrogen makes up 78% of Earth's atmosphere.
facts.wowFactor: The air you're breathing is mostly nitrogen — yet this same inert gas, rearranged into compounds, becomes TNT and dynamite.
facts.visualDescription: A calm blue sky with a pie chart overlay — 78% labeled nitrogen in pale blue, with the tiny remaining slice of other gases

variants.question: Nitrogen makes up roughly what percentage of Earth's atmosphere?
Liquid nitrogen boils at approximately what temperature?
True or false: Oxygen is the most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere.
___ makes up 78% of our atmosphere, yet its compounds include TNT and dynamite.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element is most abundant in Earth's rocky crust?
facts.explanation: Oxygen makes up 46% of Earth's crust by mass — mostly locked in silicate minerals and rocks, not as the gas we breathe. It is both the air we need and the ground we stand on. Source: Wikipedia/Oxygen
facts.statement: Oxygen is the most abundant element in Earth's crust at 46% by mass.
facts.wowFactor: Almost half of every rock, every mountain, and every grain of sand is oxygen — locked into minerals, not gas, but oxygen nonetheless.
facts.visualDescription: A cross-section of Earth's crust with oxygen atoms highlighted across rocks, sand, and soil — a glowing 46% label hovers over the stone layers

variants.question: Oxygen makes up what percentage of Earth's crust by mass?
What color is liquid oxygen?
True or false: Oxygen is the most abundant element in Earth's crust.
___ makes up 21% of the atmosphere and 46% of Earth's crust.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element is so reactive that chemists died trying to isolate it?
facts.explanation: Fluorine is the most electronegative and reactive element on the periodic table. For decades, chemists attempting to isolate it were killed or seriously injured. Henri Moissan finally succeeded in 1886 using a specialized apparatus. Source: Wikipedia/Fluorine
facts.statement: Fluorine is the most reactive element — so dangerous that it killed chemists who tried to isolate it.
facts.wowFactor: Fluorine is so reactive it can set sand and glass on fire. Multiple chemists died or were permanently blinded trying to isolate it — it took until 1886 to succeed safely.
facts.visualDescription: A pale yellow gas streams from a broken glass flask, etching the air itself, with a warning skull symbol glowing in the corrosive vapor

variants.question: Which element is the most electronegative on the periodic table?
Teflon non-stick coating is made from a compound of fluorine and which element?
True or false: Fluorine was successfully isolated before 1800.
___ is used in both toothpaste and Teflon, yet pure it is the most dangerous element.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the chemical symbol for Fluorine?
facts.explanation: Fluorine's symbol is F, from its name. It is atomic number 9 and the most reactive of all elements, yet it appears in everyday products like toothpaste. Source: Wikipedia/Fluorine
facts.statement: The chemical symbol for Fluorine is F.
facts.wowFactor: Despite being the most dangerous element to handle in its pure form, fluorine compounds are in the toothpaste you used this morning.
facts.visualDescription: A bold letter F on a periodic table tile, pale yellow background suggesting the element's dangerous gas form

variants.question: The symbol F on the periodic table stands for which element?
True or false: The chemical symbol for Fluorine is Fl.
Fluorine's chemical symbol is ___, atomic number 9.
Which halogen has the symbol F?

facts.quizQuestion: Which element was first discovered by boiling urine?
facts.explanation: In 1669, alchemist Hennig Brand discovered phosphorus by evaporating large quantities of urine, hoping to find gold. Instead he got a white waxy substance that glowed in the dark — the first element discovered in recorded history. Source: Wikipedia/Phosphorus
facts.statement: Phosphorus was discovered in 1669 by boiling urine — the first element found in recorded history.
facts.wowFactor: Phosphorus was discovered by an alchemist who boiled 50 buckets of urine looking for gold. He found a glowing white substance instead — the first element ever discovered.
facts.visualDescription: An alchemist's lab at night — a cauldron glows eerie green-white as a mysterious luminous substance drips into a vial, phosphorescent and otherworldly

variants.question: Phosphorus has which unusual historical distinction among elements?
Phosphorus is essential for which biological molecule that stores energy in cells?
True or false: White phosphorus glows in the dark.
___ was discovered in 1669 and is essential for DNA, ATP, and match heads.

facts.quizQuestion: Which yellow element is called 'brimstone' in the Bible?
facts.explanation: Sulfur is the yellow element known as brimstone, found near volcanoes and mentioned in the Bible. Its compound H2S causes the rotten-egg smell, and it is a key ingredient in gunpowder and rubber vulcanization. Source: Wikipedia/Sulfur
facts.statement: Sulfur is the yellow element known as brimstone, found near volcanoes since ancient times.
facts.wowFactor: Sulfur has been known since ancient times as brimstone — the smell of hell. That rotten-egg smell you hate near hot springs? That's hydrogen sulfide, a sulfur compound.
facts.visualDescription: Bright yellow sulfur crystals surrounding a volcanic vent, steam rising, the distinctive pale yellow deposits forming geometric crystal shapes

variants.question: Which element gives rotten eggs their distinctive smell?
Sulfur is a key ingredient in which explosive mixture known since the Middle Ages?
True or false: Sulfur is a yellow solid at room temperature.
___, called brimstone in ancient texts, is used in gunpowder and rubber vulcanization.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the chemical symbol for Chlorine?
facts.explanation: Chlorine's symbol is Cl. It is atomic number 17 and a greenish-yellow gas used to purify drinking water — yet it was deployed as a deadly poison gas in World War I. Source: Wikipedia/Chlorine
facts.statement: The chemical symbol for Chlorine is Cl.
facts.wowFactor: The same element that keeps swimming pools safe to swim in was used as a chemical weapon in World War I — context is everything in chemistry.
facts.visualDescription: A periodic table tile with Cl, greenish-yellow tinted, split image showing a clean glass of water and old WWI gas mask

variants.question: The symbol Cl stands for which element?
Chlorine combined with sodium forms which common household substance?
True or false: Chlorine is a greenish-yellow gas at room temperature.
Chlorine's symbol is ___, and it was used both to purify water and as a WWI poison gas.

facts.quizQuestion: What element category does Chlorine belong to?
facts.explanation: Chlorine is a reactive nonmetal — specifically a halogen. The halogens (F, Cl, Br, I, At) are the most reactive group of nonmetals, all tending to form salts with metals. Source: Wikipedia/Chlorine
facts.statement: Chlorine is a reactive nonmetal and a halogen.
facts.wowFactor: All halogens — fluorine, chlorine, bromine, iodine — desperately want one more electron. This hunger makes them some of the most reactive substances in chemistry.
facts.visualDescription: A glowing group 17 column on the periodic table, all four halogens highlighted, each shown in its room-temperature state: gas, gas, liquid, solid

variants.question: Which group are the halogens classified as?
What do all halogens have in common?
True or false: Chlorine is classified as a metalloid.
Chlorine, fluorine, bromine, and iodine are all members of the ___ group.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element is one of only two that are liquid at room temperature?
facts.explanation: Bromine is one of only two elements that are liquid at room temperature (the other is mercury). It is a red-brown liquid with a powerful stench — its name comes from the Greek word 'bromos' meaning stench. Source: Wikipedia/Bromine
facts.statement: Bromine is one of only two elements liquid at room temperature, along with mercury.
facts.wowFactor: Out of 118 known elements, only bromine and mercury are liquids at normal room temperature. Every other element is either a solid or a gas — these two are the oddballs.
facts.visualDescription: A sealed glass vial of dark red-brown bromine liquid with wisps of deep orange vapor above it, sitting next to a quicksilver bead of mercury

variants.question: Bromine and which other element are liquid at room temperature?
What does the name 'bromine' come from in Greek?
True or false: Bromine is a red-brown liquid at room temperature.
___ is a smelly red-brown liquid and one of only two elements liquid at room temperature.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element is added to table salt to prevent thyroid goiter?
facts.explanation: Iodine is essential for the thyroid gland to produce hormones. Deficiency causes goiter (an enlarged thyroid). Adding iodine to table salt ('iodized salt') was one of history's most effective public health interventions. Source: Wikipedia/Iodine
facts.statement: Iodine is added to table salt to prevent thyroid disorders like goiter.
facts.wowFactor: Adding a tiny amount of iodine to table salt was one of the cheapest and most effective public health measures ever — it eliminated goiter from most of the world.
facts.visualDescription: A salt shaker with 'IODIZED SALT' on the label, and beside it a glowing purple vapor rising from dark crystalline iodine — solid to gas without becoming a liquid

variants.question: Iodine deficiency is the main cause of which medical condition?
Iodine is unusual because it does what when heated from solid?
True or false: Iodine is essential for proper thyroid function.
___ is added to table salt worldwide and sublimes into a striking purple vapor.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element is named after Selene, the Greek goddess of the Moon?
facts.explanation: Selenium (Se) was discovered in 1817 by Jöns Jacob Berzelius, who named it after the Greek moon goddess Selene. Its companion element tellurium was already named after the Earth (Terra), so Berzelius named the new one after the Moon. Source: Wikipedia/Selenium
facts.statement: Selenium is named after Selene, the Greek goddess of the Moon.
facts.wowFactor: When Berzelius found selenium paired with tellurium (named after Earth), he named his discovery after the Moon — giving us an Earth-Moon pair on the periodic table.
facts.visualDescription: A crescent moon glowing over a periodic table tile marked 'Se 34', the pale silver element reflecting moonlight in its crystalline form

variants.question: Which element is paired with tellurium (Earth) and named after what celestial body?
Selenium is used in which technology that converts sunlight to electricity?
True or false: Selenium is an essential trace element for human health.
___ is named after the Moon goddess and used in solar cells.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the atomic number of Carbon?
facts.explanation: Carbon has atomic number 6 — six protons in its nucleus. This gives carbon four valence electrons, making it uniquely suited to form the complex chains and rings that underpin all known life. Source: Wikipedia/Carbon
facts.statement: Carbon has atomic number 6 and is the basis of all organic chemistry.
facts.wowFactor: Six protons. That's all it takes to create the element that forms the backbone of every living thing on Earth — from bacteria to blue whales.
facts.visualDescription: A carbon atom with 6 protons glowing at its nucleus, its four valence electrons reaching out like hands ready to bond with neighboring atoms

variants.question: Which element has atomic number 6?
Carbon-14 is used in which scientific dating technique?
True or false: Carbon forms more known compounds than any other element.
Carbon has atomic number ___, and its four valence electrons make it the backbone of all life.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the atomic number of Oxygen?
facts.explanation: Oxygen has atomic number 8 — eight protons in its nucleus. Discovered nearly simultaneously by Carl Wilhelm Scheele and Joseph Priestley in 1774, oxygen is essential for combustion and aerobic respiration. Source: Wikipedia/Oxygen
facts.statement: Oxygen has atomic number 8.
facts.wowFactor: Two scientists discovered oxygen independently in the same year, 1774 — a reminder that great discoveries happen when humanity is ready for them, not just when one person is clever enough.
facts.visualDescription: Two scientists on opposite sides of a globe, each holding a glowing vial of oxygen gas, unaware of each other — a striking moment of parallel discovery

variants.question: Which element has atomic number 8?
What is the chemical formula for ozone, a form of oxygen that blocks UV radiation?
True or false: Oxygen was discovered in 1774.
Oxygen has atomic number ___, making up 21% of the atmosphere.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element was discovered in the Sun before being found on Earth?
facts.explanation: Helium was first detected in 1868 via spectroscopy of sunlight during a solar eclipse. Its name comes from 'helios,' the Greek word for sun. It wasn't isolated on Earth until 1895.
facts.statement: Helium was discovered in the Sun's spectrum 27 years before it was found on Earth.
facts.wowFactor: Scientists found helium on the Sun before anyone knew it existed on our own planet.
facts.visualDescription: A Victorian astronomer peering through a brass telescope at a blazing orange sun, spectral rainbow lines glowing on a paper readout beside him.

variants.question: Helium was first discovered in the spectrum of which celestial body?
Helium's name comes from 'helios,' the Greek word for ___.
Helium was discovered on Earth before it was detected in the Sun.
Which group does helium belong to on the periodic table?

facts.quizQuestion: Which element has the lowest boiling point of any element, at just 4 degrees above absolute zero?
facts.explanation: Helium boils at -269°C (4 K), the lowest boiling point of any element. It remains liquid right down to near absolute zero and is the only element that will not solidify at normal pressure.
facts.statement: Helium has the lowest boiling point of any element at -269°C.
facts.wowFactor: Helium stays liquid until it's only 4 degrees above absolute zero — colder than outer space.
facts.visualDescription: A glowing blue-white flask of swirling liquid hovering just above absolute zero, frost crystals exploding off its surface in a dark lab.

variants.question: Helium has the lowest boiling point of any element on the periodic table.
Why does helium remain liquid at temperatures close to absolute zero?
variants.correctAnswer: Quantum effects prevent it from solidifying at normal pressure

variants.question: Which element has a boiling point of -269°C, the lowest of any element?
Helium is the only element that will not ___ at normal pressure.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element produces the classic red-orange glow in neon signs?
facts.explanation: Pure neon gas emits a distinctive red-orange glow when electrically excited. Other colors seen in 'neon signs' are actually produced by different gases — argon, mercury, helium, or phosphor coatings.
facts.statement: True neon gas glows red-orange; other colors in 'neon signs' use different gases.
facts.wowFactor: Most 'neon' signs contain zero neon — only the red-orange ones use actual neon gas.
facts.visualDescription: A buzzing retro diner sign with vivid red-orange tubing spelling out letters, contrasted against a rainy night-time street scene in pixel art.

variants.question: All colored neon signs use neon gas.
Neon gas glows what color when electrically excited?
Neon's name comes from the Greek word 'neos,' meaning ___.
What category of element is neon?

facts.quizQuestion: Which noble gas makes up nearly 1% of Earth's atmosphere?
facts.explanation: Argon constitutes about 0.93% of Earth's atmosphere, making it the most abundant noble gas on Earth. Its name comes from the Greek 'argos,' meaning lazy or inactive, reflecting its chemical inertness.
facts.statement: Argon makes up nearly 1% of Earth's atmosphere, more than carbon dioxide.
facts.wowFactor: There's almost 10 times more argon in the air you breathe than carbon dioxide.
facts.visualDescription: A cross-section diagram of Earth's sky layers with glowing labels, argon highlighted in a thick purple band dwarfing a tiny sliver labeled CO2.

variants.question: Argon's name comes from the Greek word meaning lazy or ___.
Argon is more abundant in Earth's atmosphere than carbon dioxide.
Which element makes up 0.93% of Earth's atmosphere?
Why is argon used in welding and light bulbs?
variants.correctAnswer: It prevents oxidation as an inert shield gas

facts.quizQuestion: Which noble gas was used to define the meter from 1960 to 1983?
facts.explanation: From 1960 to 1983, the meter was officially defined as 1,650,763.73 wavelengths of orange-red light emitted by krypton-86. Its name comes from the Greek 'kryptos,' meaning hidden — not Superman's planet.
facts.statement: From 1960–1983, the official meter was defined by the wavelength of krypton-86 light.
facts.wowFactor: Your school ruler was once officially defined by the glow of a single noble gas isotope.
facts.visualDescription: A glowing orange-red light beam shooting between two rulers marked with wavelength tick marks, a periodic table square for Kr in the background.

variants.question: Krypton is named after Superman's home planet.
Krypton's name comes from the Greek word 'kryptos,' meaning ___.
The meter was defined by the wavelength of light from which element (1960–1983)?
What category of element is krypton?

facts.quizQuestion: Which noble gas was the first proven to form a stable chemical compound (1962)?
facts.explanation: In 1962, Neil Bartlett became the first person to make a noble gas compound when he synthesized xenon hexafluoroplatinate. This shattered the long-held belief that noble gases could not react chemically.
facts.statement: Xenon became the first noble gas to form a stable compound in 1962, breaking a chemistry dogma.
facts.wowFactor: For decades chemists insisted noble gases were completely unreactive — xenon proved them all wrong.
facts.visualDescription: A 1960s lab scientist triumphantly holding up a glowing yellow crystal vial, periodic table poster on the wall with noble gases all crossed out then un-crossed.

variants.question: Neil Bartlett in 1962 first made a stable compound from which noble gas?
Xenon's name comes from the Greek word 'xenos,' meaning stranger or ___.
Noble gases were once believed to be completely unable to form chemical compounds.
Xenon is also used in spacecraft propulsion as an ion drive fuel. Why is it well-suited?
variants.correctAnswer: It is heavy and inert, ideal for ionization thrust
distractors: It is the lightest noble gas

facts.quizQuestion: Which radioactive noble gas is the second leading cause of lung cancer?
facts.explanation: Radon is a naturally radioactive gas formed by the decay of uranium in rocks and soil. It seeps into buildings and is the second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking, responsible for ~21,000 deaths per year in the US.
facts.statement: Radon gas, seeping from soil into basements, is the second leading cause of lung cancer.
facts.wowFactor: A deadly radioactive gas is silently seeping into millions of homes right now — and most people have never tested for it.
facts.visualDescription: A glowing green mist rising up through basement floor cracks at night, a danger symbol icon pulsing, a suburban house cross-section in pixel art.

variants.question: Radon is safe because noble gases are chemically inert.
What is the second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking?
Where does environmental radon gas originally come from?
variants.correctAnswer: Decay of uranium in rocks and soil

variants.question: Radon forms naturally as a decay product of ___ in rocks.

facts.quizQuestion: Which alkali metal is the lightest solid element and floats on water?
facts.explanation: Lithium has a density of only 0.534 g/cm³ — less than half the density of water — making it the lightest solid element. It floats on water and reacts with it, producing lithium hydroxide and hydrogen gas.
facts.statement: Lithium is so light it floats on water, with a density less than half that of water.
facts.wowFactor: The same ultra-light metal that powers your phone can literally float on water.
facts.visualDescription: A shiny silver cube bobbing on water's surface while fizzing, a smartphone battery icon floating nearby, in bright pixel art colors.

variants.question: Lithium is so dense it sinks to the bottom of water.
What common modern technology relies heavily on lithium?
Which element is the lightest solid on the periodic table?
Lithium burns with a ___ flame color.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the chemical symbol for sodium?
facts.explanation: Sodium's symbol Na comes from 'Natrium,' its Latin name. Sodium is essential for nerve signaling, forms table salt (NaCl), and burns with a vivid yellow flame — the reason sodium street lamps glow yellow.
facts.statement: Sodium's symbol Na comes from 'Natrium,' its Latin name, not from 'sodium.'
facts.wowFactor: Every time you use table salt, you're holding a compound of a metal that explodes in water.
facts.visualDescription: A bright yellow flare erupting from a small piece of silver metal dropping into water, a salt shaker tipping over beside it in a pixel art kitchen.

variants.question: The symbol Na for sodium comes from which Latin name?
Sodium burns with a vivid ___ flame.
Sodium's chemical symbol is 'So.'
What essential biological role does sodium play in the human body?

facts.quizQuestion: What is the chemical symbol for potassium?
facts.explanation: Potassium's symbol K comes from 'Kalium,' its medieval Latin name derived from Arabic 'al-qali' (alkali). It was isolated by Humphry Davy in 1807, the same year he isolated sodium.
facts.statement: Potassium's symbol K comes from 'Kalium,' a Latin name derived from the Arabic word for alkali.
facts.wowFactor: Humphry Davy isolated both sodium and potassium in the same year — and potassium was the first metal ever isolated by electrolysis.
facts.visualDescription: A Victorian scientist at an electrolysis apparatus with tiny sparkling metal beads collecting at an electrode, a banana and a potassium label nearby.

variants.question: The symbol K for potassium comes from which Latin name?
Potassium reacts more violently with water than sodium does.
Which fruit is famously associated with potassium?
Potassium burns with a ___ flame color.

facts.quizQuestion: At what temperature in °C does caesium melt (roughly body temperature)?
facts.explanation: Caesium melts at just 28.5°C, very close to human body temperature (37°C). It is a golden-colored, highly reactive alkali metal and is the most reactive of all stable elements.
facts.statement: Caesium melts at 28°C — nearly the same as human body temperature.
facts.wowFactor: Caesium is so reactive it melts in your hand before it explodes from touching your skin's moisture.
facts.visualDescription: A golden liquid metal pooling in a gloved palm, temperature gauge showing 28°C, warning symbols flashing, in a pixel art lab scene.

variants.question: Caesium could theoretically melt if held in a warm human hand.
What critical modern timekeeping device uses caesium?
Which alkali metal is considered the most reactive of all stable elements?
Caesium atomic clocks are used to define the international standard ___.

facts.quizQuestion: Which naturally occurring element has only ~30 grams present in all of Earth's crust?
facts.explanation: Francium is so unstable (half-life ~22 minutes) that at any moment only about 20–30 grams exist in Earth's entire crust. It was discovered by French physicist Marguerite Perey in 1939 and named after France.
facts.statement: At any moment, only about 30 grams of francium exist in all of Earth's crust.
facts.wowFactor: The rarest naturally occurring element on Earth — the entire planet holds less of it than a golf ball weighs.
facts.visualDescription: A minuscule glowing speck on a world globe map labeled '~30g total,' a countdown timer showing 22 minutes, French flag colors in the background.

variants.question: Francium is the most unstable naturally occurring element, with a half-life of about 22 minutes.
Francium was discovered in 1939 by a physicist from which country?
Francium was discovered by Marguerite ___ in 1939.
What category of element is francium?

facts.quizQuestion: Which alkaline earth metal burns with a brilliant white flame used in flares and fireworks?
facts.explanation: Magnesium burns with an intensely bright white flame reaching ~3,100°C, so bright it can damage eyesight. It is used in emergency flares, incendiary devices, and fireworks. It also cannot be extinguished with water — water makes it burn hotter.
facts.statement: Magnesium burns so brightly you cannot look directly at it, and water makes the fire worse.
facts.wowFactor: Trying to extinguish a magnesium fire with water is like throwing gasoline on it — the metal tears oxygen from the water molecules.
facts.visualDescription: A blindingly bright white flame erupting from a silver ribbon on a lab bench, safety goggles icon with a warning, sparks flying everywhere in pixel art.

variants.question: Pouring water on a magnesium fire will extinguish it safely.
Why is magnesium essential for plant life?
variants.correctAnswer: It is the central atom in chlorophyll molecules

variants.question: Which element is the central metal atom in chlorophyll?
Magnesium is the ___ most abundant element in Earth's crust.

facts.quizQuestion: Which alkaline earth metal is the most abundant metal in the human body?
facts.explanation: The average human body contains about 1 kg of calcium, mostly stored in bones and teeth. Calcium is also essential for muscle contraction, including the heart, and for nerve signal transmission.
facts.statement: Calcium is the most abundant metal in the human body — about 1 kg in an adult, mostly in bones.
facts.wowFactor: Your skeleton is basically a calcium battery — your body continuously dissolves and rebuilds it to regulate calcium levels in your blood.
facts.visualDescription: A glowing human skeleton with highlighted bone segments labeled 'Ca,' milk carton and cheese beside it, heart pulsing nearby in pixel art.

variants.question: Calcium is essential for muscle contraction, including the heart.
Limestone, chalk, and marble are all forms of which calcium compound?
Approximately how much calcium does an adult human body contain?
Most calcium in the human body is stored in ___ and teeth.

facts.quizQuestion: Which alkaline earth metal produces the brilliant red color in fireworks?
facts.explanation: Strontium salts produce a brilliant crimson-red flame and are the primary source of red in fireworks. Strontium was named after Strontian, a village in Scotland where the ore strontianite was first discovered.
facts.statement: The brilliant red colors in fireworks come from strontium salts burning at high temperature.
facts.wowFactor: Every red burst you've ever seen in a fireworks show was strontium — named after a tiny village in Scotland.
facts.visualDescription: A night sky exploding with brilliant red fireworks bursts, a pixel art Scottish highland village visible below, element symbol Sr glowing in the sparks.

variants.question: Strontium was named after a village in which country?
Radioactive strontium-90 from nuclear fallout is dangerous because it mimics ___ in bones.
Strontium produces green colors in fireworks.
What category of element is strontium?

facts.quizQuestion: Which alkaline earth metal is swallowed as a 'meal' to X-ray the digestive tract?
facts.explanation: Barium sulfate is opaque to X-rays and is completely insoluble (safe to swallow). Patients drink a 'barium meal' before digestive X-rays so the gut appears white on the image, revealing blockages or ulcers.
facts.statement: Patients swallow a barium sulfate suspension to make their digestive tract visible on X-rays.
facts.wowFactor: Swallowing a heavy metal compound sounds terrifying, but barium sulfate is so chemically inert it passes through your body completely unchanged.
facts.visualDescription: An X-ray image glowing on a lightbox showing a white stomach outline, a patient holding a cup labeled 'barium meal,' pixel art hospital setting.

variants.question: Barium compounds are always toxic and dangerous to consume.
Barium's name comes from the Greek 'barys,' meaning ___.
What color does barium produce in fireworks?
Which element's sulfate compound is used in medical gastrointestinal X-rays?

facts.quizQuestion: Which radioactive element was painted on watch dials, slowly killing the 'Radium Girls'?
facts.explanation: In the 1920s, young women at the US Radium Corporation painted luminous watch dials with radium paint and were told it was safe. Many developed radiation sickness, jaw necrosis, and cancer. Their legal battle led to foundational worker safety laws.
facts.statement: The 'Radium Girls' painted luminous watch dials with radium paint in the 1920s, not knowing it would kill them.
facts.wowFactor: The Radium Girls were told to lick their brushes to get a finer point — their supervisors knew the danger and said nothing.
facts.visualDescription: A 1920s factory worker painting a clock dial with glowing green paint, her face lit eerie green, a skull symbol faintly visible in the paint bucket.

variants.question: Marie Curie's personal notebooks are still radioactive today.
Marie and Pierre Curie discovered which radioactive alkaline earth metal in 1898?
What visible phenomenon does radium produce due to its radioactivity?
variants.correctAnswer: It glows blue-green in the dark
distractors: It produces heat visible in infrared

variants.question: The Radium Girls' legal battles directly led to new ___ safety laws in the US.

facts.quizQuestion: Which lightweight metal is nearly transparent to X-rays and used as X-ray tube windows?
facts.explanation: Beryllium is nearly transparent to X-rays due to its low atomic number, making it ideal for X-ray tube windows. It is also extremely light, stiff, and toxic as dust. Emeralds and aquamarines are beryllium minerals.
facts.statement: Beryllium is nearly transparent to X-rays and forms the gemstones emerald and aquamarine.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element, named after a mythological king, is inside nearly every smartphone as tiny capacitors?
facts.explanation: Tantalum is used in miniaturized capacitors found in virtually all smartphones, laptops, and electronics. Named after Tantalus of Greek myth (punished by being surrounded by food and water he couldn't reach), tantalum ore (coltan) is a major conflict mineral.
facts.statement: Tantalum capacitors are inside nearly every smartphone and laptop — making this mythologically-named metal essential to modern electronics.
facts.wowFactor: Every time you use a smartphone, you're using tantalum — an element named after a Greek king condemned to eternal frustration.
facts.visualDescription: A microscopic view of a tantalum capacitor chip beside a smartphone circuit board, with a ghostly image of the tortured Tantalus in water.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element is the basis of almost all modern computer chips?
facts.explanation: Silicon is the second most abundant element in Earth's crust and the foundation of semiconductor technology. Silicon Valley in California takes its name from this element.
facts.statement: Silicon is the element that computer chips and nearly all modern electronics are built from.
facts.wowFactor: Without silicon, none of your devices would exist — every CPU, RAM chip, and transistor is built from this abundant metalloid.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element did Mendeleev predict and call 'eka-silicon' before its discovery?
facts.explanation: Mendeleev predicted the existence, properties, and approximate atomic weight of germanium in 1871 — 15 years before Clemens Winkler discovered it in 1886. This prediction powerfully validated the periodic table.
facts.statement: Germanium was famously predicted by Mendeleev as 'eka-silicon' before it was discovered, dramatically validating the periodic table.
facts.wowFactor: Mendeleev predicted Germanium's density, melting point, and properties with stunning accuracy 15 years before it was found — proof his periodic table was a true law of nature.
facts.visualDescription: Ancient scroll with handwritten periodic table predictions, with a glowing element box labeled 'eka-silicon' transforming into a shining crystal labeled Ge.

facts.quizQuestion: Which metal was once more precious than gold, used for Napoleon III's finest cutlery?
facts.explanation: Before the Hall-Heroult electrolytic process was invented in 1886, aluminium was incredibly difficult to refine and cost more per ounce than gold. Napoleon III reserved aluminium cutlery for his most honored guests while lesser guests used gold.
facts.statement: Aluminium was once more precious than gold — Napoleon III served honored guests with aluminium cutlery while lesser guests had to make do with gold.
facts.wowFactor: The Washington Monument's aluminum cap (1884) was a statement of ultimate luxury — the same metal you throw away in soda cans was once the most prized metal on Earth.
facts.visualDescription: Elegant 19th-century banquet table with gleaming silver-white cutlery labeled 'aluminium' at the head, gold cutlery for lesser guests further down.

facts.quizQuestion: Which metal melts at 29.8°C, meaning it liquefies in a warm human hand?
facts.explanation: Gallium has a melting point of just 29.8°C (85.6°F), just below normal body temperature of 37°C. A solid gallium object placed in a warm hand will slowly melt into a silver liquid puddle.
facts.statement: Gallium melts at 29.8°C — just hold a solid piece in your warm hand and watch it slowly dissolve into shimmering liquid metal.
facts.wowFactor: A classic prank: make a spoon from gallium and offer it for stirring hot tea — it silently melts away in the cup. Gallium was also predicted by Mendeleev as 'eka-aluminium' before its 1875 discovery.
facts.visualDescription: A gloved hand holding a shiny silvery-blue solid that is visibly melting into a liquid puddle, thermometer nearby showing 30 degrees.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the chemical symbol for Lead, derived from the Latin word that also gives us 'plumbing'?
facts.explanation: Lead's symbol Pb comes from the Latin word 'plumbum'. The Romans used lead extensively for water pipes, and the Latin root plumbum gives us the English word 'plumbing' and the profession 'plumber'.
facts.statement: Lead's symbol Pb comes from Latin 'plumbum' — the same root that gives us the word 'plumbing', since Romans built their water pipes from lead.
facts.wowFactor: Every time you call a plumber, you're invoking lead's Latin name. The Romans' love of lead pipes may have contributed to widespread lead poisoning across the empire.
facts.visualDescription: Ancient Roman aqueduct with lead pipes labeled 'plumbum', water flowing through, with a modern plumber's wrench overlaid to show the word connection.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the unusual chemical symbol for Antimony, derived from the Latin 'stibium'?
facts.explanation: Antimony's symbol Sb comes from Latin 'stibium', which itself derives from the Ancient Greek word for the mineral stibnite. Ancient Egyptians used ground antimony sulfide as kohl eye makeup for thousands of years.
facts.statement: Antimony's symbol Sb comes from the Latin 'stibium' — ancient Egyptians used antimony sulfide as black eye makeup (kohl) for thousands of years.
facts.wowFactor: Every eyeliner pencil traces its heritage to ancient antimony-based kohl. Cleopatra's iconic black eye makeup was likely made from this metalloid's sulfide compound.

facts.quizQuestion: Which metalloid was historically called 'the king of poisons and the poison of kings'?
facts.explanation: Arsenic (As, atomic number 33) was a notorious poison in medieval and Renaissance Europe, nicknamed 'inheritance powder' because it was used to hasten inheritance. It was nearly undetectable until the Marsh test was developed in 1836.
facts.statement: Arsenic earned the nickname 'inheritance powder' in Renaissance Europe — colorless, tasteless, and nearly undetectable, it was the favored poison of scheming heirs.
facts.wowFactor: Victorian-era wallpapers colored with Scheele's Green (copper arsenite) were so toxic that damp rooms could release arsenic vapors — historians believe this may have contributed to Napoleon's death in his damp St. Helena exile.
facts.visualDescription: Victorian bedroom with lush green wallpaper, a skull-and-crossbones faintly visible in the floral pattern, candlelight casting eerie green shadows.

facts.quizQuestion: Which heavy metal forms stunning iridescent rainbow-colored staircase crystals when slowly cooled?
facts.explanation: Bismuth (Bi, atomic number 83) forms spectacular hopper crystals with a staircase structure. A thin oxide layer forms as it cools, and light interference in this layer produces brilliant rainbow iridescent colors — each crystal is unique.
facts.statement: Bismuth grows stunning rainbow-colored hopper crystals — the iridescent colors come from thin-film interference in the oxide layer that forms as the metal cools.
facts.wowFactor: Bismuth is the least toxic heavy metal and has an absurdly long half-life for radioactive decay: 1.9 x 10^19 years — over a billion times the age of the universe. It's practically stable.
facts.visualDescription: Staircase-shaped metallic crystal with vivid rainbow iridescence — blues purples golds — sitting on a dark slate surface, glinting like a miniature geometric palace.

facts.quizQuestion: Which synthetic radioactive element is found in virtually every household smoke detector?
facts.explanation: Americium-241 is used in ionization-type smoke detectors. A tiny amount (about 1 microgram) ionizes air between two electrodes; smoke disrupts the ion current and triggers the alarm. It was first synthesized at Berkeley in 1944.
facts.statement: Americium-241, a synthetic radioactive element, sits in the ionization chamber of nearly every smoke detector in the world — there's probably one in your ceiling right now.
facts.wowFactor: Most people are shocked to learn they live with a radioactive actinide element every day. The amount is tiny (1 microgram) and completely safe — the aluminum housing contains all radiation.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section of a white smoke detector showing a small glowing chamber labeled Am-241, smoke particles drifting through triggering a red alarm indicator.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element did Marie Curie name after her native country Poland?
facts.explanation: Marie Curie discovered polonium in 1898 and named it after Poland, her homeland, which was then under Russian, Prussian, and Austrian partition. It was a political act of defiance and national pride. Polonium-210 was used to poison Alexander Litvinenko in 2006.
facts.statement: Marie Curie named Polonium after Poland — at the time her homeland was erased from maps, partitioned between empires. Naming an element after it was an act of defiance.
facts.wowFactor: Polonium-210 is one of the most toxic substances known. In 2006, former Russian spy Alexander Litvinenko was assassinated with it — a microscopic amount delivered in tea was fatal.
facts.visualDescription: Black-and-white portrait style of a female scientist beside a glowing green vial labeled Po, a faded map of Poland in the background, radiation hazard symbol overlaid.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the atomic number of Uranium, the heaviest naturally occurring element?
facts.explanation: Uranium (U) has atomic number 92 and is the heaviest element found naturally in significant quantities in Earth's crust. It was named after the planet Uranus and discovered by Martin Heinrich Klaproth in 1789.
facts.statement: Uranium has atomic number 92 and is more abundant in Earth's crust than tin or silver — despite its association with nuclear danger, it's surprisingly common.
facts.wowFactor: Uranium is more common in Earth's crust than gold, silver, or tin. The nuclear age runs on a surprisingly ordinary element — it's even found naturally in seawater.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element powered both the Fat Man atomic bomb and the Voyager space probes?
facts.explanation: Plutonium-239 was used in the Fat Man bomb dropped on Nagasaki in 1945. Plutonium-238 (different isotope) powers RTG (radioisotope thermoelectric generator) units on space probes like Voyager, Cassini, and Curiosity rover — converting its radioactive decay heat to electricity.
facts.statement: Plutonium powered the most destructive bomb in WWII and also quietly powers the Voyager probes still traveling through interstellar space — a dual legacy of destruction and exploration.
facts.wowFactor: Voyager 1, now billions of miles from Earth, is still transmitting because of plutonium's heat. The same element that leveled Nagasaki now whispers data back across the cosmos.
facts.visualDescription: Split scene: atomic bomb mushroom cloud on left, Voyager probe with golden record floating in starfield on right, Pu symbol at center connecting both.

facts.quizQuestion: Which lanthanide element makes the strongest permanent magnets used in headphones and electric motors?
facts.explanation: Neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB) magnets are the strongest type of permanent magnet known. They are used in headphones, hard drives, electric vehicle motors, wind turbines, and MRI machines. Neodymium was discovered in 1885.
facts.statement: Neodymium magnets are the strongest permanent magnets known — the tiny magnet in your headphones or the massive ones in EV motors are both neodymium-iron-boron alloys.
facts.wowFactor: A neodymium magnet the size of a coin can lift hundreds of times its own weight. The global transition to electric vehicles is creating massive demand for neodymium — it's a critical mineral of the green energy revolution.
facts.visualDescription: Two silver horseshoe-shaped magnets snapping together with visible force lines, electric car and wind turbine in background, Nd symbol glowing in center.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element named after Marie and Pierre Curie is so radioactive it glows in the dark?
facts.explanation: Curium (Cm, atomic number 96) was named in honor of Marie and Pierre Curie. It is intensely radioactive — solid curium emits enough alpha radiation to heat itself and glow faint blue-purple in darkness. It is used in alpha particle X-ray spectrometers on Mars rovers.
facts.statement: Curium glows in the dark from its own radioactivity — it was named for Marie and Pierre Curie, and a speck of it sits on the Mars rovers analyzing rocks with X-ray spectroscopy.
facts.wowFactor: Curium is so intensely radioactive that a small sample heats itself to incandescence and glows visibly in the dark. The Curiosity rover's chemistry lab contains curium-244 — Marie Curie's namesake element is currently on Mars.
facts.visualDescription: Dark laboratory with a small glowing blue-purple sphere of metal emitting visible light, Mars landscape visible through a window, Cm symbol above.

facts.quizQuestion: Which toxic post-transition metal was called 'the poisoner's poison' because it is tasteless, odorless, and water-soluble?
facts.explanation: Thallium is extremely toxic and has been used in numerous murder cases. It causes hair loss, nerve damage, and organ failure while symptoms mimic other diseases — making it very hard to diagnose. Agatha Christie referenced thallium poisoning in her detective fiction.
facts.statement: Thallium was called 'the poisoner's poison' — tasteless, odorless, water-soluble, and causing symptoms that mimic other diseases, making it almost undetectable until modern toxicology.
facts.wowFactor: Thallium's name comes from the Greek 'thallos' (green shoot) because it was discovered via a brilliant green spectral emission line. The deadliest elements often have the most beautiful discovery stories.
facts.visualDescription: Chemistry spectroscope showing a vivid green spectral line against a dark background, a small metallic vial labeled Tl nearby, 'discovered by green light' caption.

facts.quizQuestion: Which rare metal, used in indium tin oxide coatings, makes modern touchscreens and LCD displays possible?
facts.explanation: Indium tin oxide (ITO) is a transparent conductor used in LCD displays, touchscreens, and solar cells. Indium (In, atomic number 49) is relatively rare and most world supply comes from zinc ore processing. It also makes a distinctive 'tin cry' when bent.
facts.statement: Every time you swipe a touchscreen, you're interacting with indium tin oxide — the transparent conducting film that makes touch detection possible on LCDs and phones.
facts.wowFactor: Indium is so rare that recycling old smartphones for their indium is economically viable. The touchscreen economy has made this obscure metal strategically critical — and supplies may be limited.

facts.quizQuestion: Which rare metalloid causes garlic-like breath when even tiny amounts are absorbed by the skin?
facts.explanation: Tellurium (Te, atomic number 52) is notorious among chemists because minute skin absorption causes the body to produce dimethyl telluride, a volatile compound exhaled from the lungs that causes a persistent and powerful garlic-like odor lasting weeks.
facts.statement: Tellurium is feared in chemistry labs: even tiny amounts absorbed through skin cause weeks of garlic-like breath from dimethyl telluride the body produces from it.
facts.wowFactor: Tellurium is named after Earth (Latin 'tellus') and is one of the rarest stable solid elements in the Earth's crust — rarer than platinum. Despite this, it's used in solar panels and phase-change memory chips.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the atomic number of Thorium, the element named after the Norse god of thunder?
facts.explanation: Thorium (Th) has atomic number 90 and was named after Thor, the Norse god of thunder, by discoverer Jons Jacob Berzelius in 1828. It is approximately 3 times more abundant than uranium in Earth's crust and is considered a potential alternative nuclear fuel.
facts.statement: Thorium (atomic number 90) is named after Thor — the Norse god of thunder. It is 3 times more abundant than uranium and could power thorium-cycle nuclear reactors.
facts.wowFactor: India, which has vast thorium reserves, has been actively developing thorium reactors for decades. A thorium-powered world could have abundant, cleaner nuclear energy with far less long-lived waste than uranium reactors.

facts.quizQuestion: Which metalloid is essential for plant growth, used in Pyrex glass, and is nearly as hard as diamond at 9.3 on the Mohs scale?
facts.explanation: Boron (B, atomic number 5) in its pure crystalline form scores 9.3 on the Mohs hardness scale — almost as hard as diamond (10). It is essential for plant cell wall formation and is the key ingredient in borosilicate glass (Pyrex) and borax cleaning products.
facts.statement: Pure crystalline boron scores 9.3 on the Mohs hardness scale — nearly as hard as diamond — yet borax (a boron compound) is a gentle household cleaner and plants cannot survive without it.
facts.wowFactor: Boron has a split personality: it's almost as hard as diamond in pure crystal form, yet its compounds include the soft white powder in laundry detergents and the compound that makes plants grow. Boron compounds were also used in WWII-era rocket fuels.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side comparison: shiny black crystalline boron shard next to a white box of borax powder, a healthy green plant growing between them, Mohs scale showing 9.3.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the atomic number of Lead, the dense metal whose Latin name gave us the word 'plumbing'?
facts.explanation: Lead (Pb) has atomic number 82. Its symbol comes from the Latin 'plumbum', which also gives us the words 'plumbing' and 'plumber', since Romans built water pipes from lead. Lead has been known and used since antiquity.
facts.statement: Lead has atomic number 82 and symbol Pb from Latin 'plumbum' — the same root as 'plumbing', since Roman water infrastructure relied heavily on lead pipes.
facts.wowFactor: Lead-206, lead-207, and lead-208 are all stable end-products of uranium and thorium radioactive decay chains. This is why chemists could use lead isotope ratios to accurately date the age of Earth's oldest rocks.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section diagram of Roman lead pipe labeled 'Plumbum' connected to ancient aqueduct stonework, with the atomic structure Pb/82 displayed above.

facts.quizQuestion: Sodium, potassium, and lithium all belong to which element category?
facts.explanation: Alkali metals (Group 1) are the most reactive metals — they explode on contact with water and must be stored in oil.
facts.statement: Sodium, potassium, and lithium are alkali metals.
facts.wowFactor: Drop a chunk of sodium in water and it bursts into flames — these metals are so reactive they literally explode on contact with water.

facts.quizQuestion: Iron, copper, gold, and silver all belong to which element category?
facts.explanation: Transition metals (Groups 3-12) include most of the well-known metals. They are generally hard, dense, and good conductors.
facts.statement: Iron, copper, gold, and silver are transition metals.
facts.wowFactor: The transition metals include almost every metal you can name — iron, gold, silver, copper, zinc, nickel, chromium, titanium, and platinum are all in this one category.

facts.quizQuestion: Silicon and germanium are examples of which element category — elements with properties between metals and nonmetals?
facts.explanation: Metalloids have properties intermediate between metals and nonmetals. Silicon is the most famous metalloid — it is the basis of all modern computer chips.
facts.statement: Silicon and germanium are metalloids.
facts.wowFactor: Metalloids are the reason computers exist — silicon is neither a good conductor nor a good insulator, making it perfect for controlling electrical signals in microchips.
